sanskrit_devanagari:
न कर्तृत्वं न कर्माणि लोकस्य सृजति प्रभुः ।
न कर्मफलसंयोगं स्वभावस्तु प्रवर्तते ॥ १४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
na kartṛtvaṁ na karmāṇi lokasya sṛjati prabhuḥ |
na karma-phala-saṁyogaṁ svabhāvas tu pravartate || 14 ||

anvaya.meaning: nor union with the fruits of action

translations.verbatim_quote: The embodied spirit, master of the city of his body, does not create activities, nor does he induce people to act, nor does he create the fruits of action. All this is enacted by the modes of material nature.

The embodied spirit, master of the city of his body, does not create activities, nor does he induce people to act, nor does he create the fruits of action. All this is enacted by the modes of material nature.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1254 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The living entity, as will be explained in the Seventh Chapter, is one of the energies or natures of the Supreme Lord but is distinct from matter, which is another nature – called inferior – of the Lord.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.14 Prabhuh, the Self; na srjati, does not create; lokasya, for anyone; kartrtvam, agentship, by saying ‘Do this’; or even karmani, any objects-such objects as chariot, pot, palace, etc.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-14-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: the prabhu (the Self/Ātman, the master of the body-mind) does not create agency for the world (does not say 'do this'), nor does it create actions (objects such as chariots, pots), nor union-with-the-fruits-of-action. Rather, svabhāva (own-nature, the projection of māyā / avidyā) operates. Shankara's Advaita reading: the Self is not the agent; agency is misattributed to the Self because of avidyā.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.14 When the world of embodied selves exists in conjunction with the Prakrti in the form of gods, animals, men, immobile things etc., the master (Prabhu i.e., the Jiva who is the master of the body), who is not subject to Karma and is established in Its own essential nature, does not bring about: (…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-14-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: the prabhu (the jīva, the master of the body, who is established in its own essential nature, not subject to karma) does not bring about agency, action, or union-with-fruits-of-action for the world (the field of body-and-its-environment). Rather, svabhāva — i.e., the conjunction of the self with prakṛti rooted in beginningless karma — operates. Ramanuja's reading: the self proper does not create agency; the karmically-conditioned conjunction does.

positions.rendering: the embodied spirit, master of the city of his body
Advaita: the Self / Ātman, who does not create agency for the world; svabhāva = māyā/avidyā operates
Vishishtadvaita: the jīva established in its essential nature; svabhāva = the karmic conjunction operates

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Major sectarian disagreement. Shankara reads prabhu as the absolute Self (since later commentators in this lineage identify it with brahman); Ramanuja reads it as the individual jīva in its essential self-nature; some readings (e.g. some Madhva) read it as the Lord (Vishnu). All preserve the structural claim: agency-and-action-and-fruit are not authored by the prabhu but by svabhāva.

literal_meaning: The Lord/master does not create agency, nor actions, nor union with the fruits of action, for the world. Rather, own-nature operates.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna makes a metaphysical claim about agency. The prabhu (Lord / master / embodied Self, depending on tradition) does not create kartṛtva (agency), karma (action), or karma-phala-saṁyoga (union with fruits) for the world. Rather, svabhāva — own-nature, structural propensity — operates. The verse is read across traditions as the foundation for the non-doership doctrine of 5.8-5.9. The engineering analog is structural-not-hero-narrative; the verse exceeds this.
